oneSentenceDescription: The Réseau express métropolitain (REM) is a 67-kilometer automated light metro system serving the Greater Montreal area, connecting downtown Montreal to the South Shore, West Island, North Shore, and Montréal-Trudeau International Airport.
ownershipDetails: Firm: CDPQ Infra | Fund: CDPQ Infra | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Réseau express métropolitain is an automated light-metro system serving Greater Montréal. Its users are public-transit riders travelling across the Montréal region rather than freight or utility customers. The business is asset-heavy and concession-like because value depends on rail infrastructure, stations, rolling stock, and long-term public-transit operations. Official project materials describe REM as a 67-kilometer network with 26 stations. Its principal operating footprint is in Québec. CDPQ Infra launched the project in 2016, opened the first operating section in 2023, and has continued staged commissioning of additional segments through 2025.
historicalMilestones: 2016: CDPQ Infra launched the REM project for Greater Montréal.
2023-07-31: The first REM operating section opened on the South Shore.
2025: Additional segments were commissioned as staged openings continued across the network.
2025-2026: REM continued expanding toward its full 67-kilometer, 26-station footprint.

principalLocationOfOperations: Kansas, Oklahoma, Missouri, Wyoming, Colorado, Texas, Nebraska, and Kentucky
ownershipDetails: Firm: CDPQ | Fund: CDPQ Infrastructure | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
Firm: GE Energy Financial Services | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Southern Star Central Gas Pipeline owns and operates an interstate natural-gas transmission and storage system in the central United States. Its customers include gas utilities, producers, marketers, and power generators that contract for transportation and storage capacity. The business is asset-heavy and largely contracted because value depends on long-lived pipeline and storage infrastructure regulated by FERC. Company materials state that Southern Star marked 120 years of service in 2024, implying a 1904 founding date for the historical system. Its operating footprint spans the Mid-Continent region, including Kansas, Oklahoma, Missouri, Wyoming, Colorado, Texas, Nebraska, and Kentucky. CDPQ and GE Energy Financial Services announced the acquisition of Southern Star in 2015, and public materials reviewed do not disclose the parties' current ownership percentages.
historicalMilestones: 1904: Southern Star's 120-year anniversary materials point to 1904 as the origin year of the system.
2015-11-12: CDPQ and GE Energy Financial Services announced their partnership to acquire Southern Star Central Corp.
2016: The acquisition closed and Southern Star continued operating under new ownership.
2024: Southern Star celebrated 120 years of service.
2024-2026: The company continued operating interstate pipeline and storage assets across the Mid-Continent region.

profileCompanyName: Student Transportation of America and Canada
principalLocationOfOperations: 23 US states, five Canadian provinces, and one territory
ownershipDetails: Firm: CDPQ | Fund: CDPQ Infrastructure | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Student Transportation of America and Canada provides school-bus and related student transportation services. Its customers are school districts, provincial and state education authorities, and other public-sector transportation buyers that contract for managed route service rather than consumer transit. The business is asset-heavy and contract-based because value depends on fleet assets, safety operations, and long-term transportation contracts. CDPQ has described the company as the third-largest school transportation operator in North America, with approximately 21,000 vehicles across 23 US states, five provinces, and one territory. Its principal operating footprint spans the United States and Canada. CDPQ first invested in 2004 and led the take-private acquisition of Student Transportation Inc. in 2018.
historicalMilestones: 1997: Student Transportation was founded.
2004: CDPQ made its initial investment in the business.
2018-04-24: Student Transportation Inc. announced that it would be acquired by a group of investors led by CDPQ.
2018-2026: The business continued operating a large North American school-transportation fleet under private ownership.

ownershipDetails: Firm: CDPQ | Fund: CDPQ Infrastructure | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
Firm: Pattern Energy Group LP | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: SunZia Wind and Transmission is a combined renewable-generation and transmission project linking wind resources in New Mexico to load centers in the US Southwest. Its counterparties include wholesale power buyers and transmission users rather than retail utility customers alone. The project is highly asset-heavy because value depends on 3.5 gigawatts of wind generation, a 550-mile transmission system, and associated long-term power and transmission arrangements. Pattern Energy described the financing closed in 2023 as the largest clean-energy infrastructure project financing in US history. Its principal operating footprint spans New Mexico, Arizona, and California. CDPQ has reported holdings related to SunZia in its infrastructure portfolio, while public sources reviewed do not disclose the current ownership percentages among the project sponsors.
historicalMilestones: 2023-12-27: Pattern Energy announced the closing of approximately $11 billion of financing for SunZia Wind and Transmission.
2023: Pattern described the project as 3.5 GW of wind generation paired with a 550-mile transmission line.
2024: CDPQ reported SunZia-related holdings in its additional information disclosures.
2026: Pattern continued targeting project completion and commercial operation during 2026.

ownershipDetails: Firm: CDPQ Infra | Fund: CDPQ Infra | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: TramCité is a proposed tramway and mobility project for Québec City. Its users are expected to be public-transit riders rather than freight, power, or communications customers. The project is development-stage and infrastructure-heavy because value depends on route design, civil works, rolling stock, and future operations and maintenance arrangements. CDPQ Infra materials describe the project at approximately 19 kilometers. Its principal operating footprint is in Québec. CDPQ Infra is overseeing the development process, and public materials reviewed do not disclose a separate external equity ownership structure for the project at this stage.
historicalMilestones: 2024: Governments and CDPQ Infra advanced the TramCité mobility project in Québec City.
2025: CDPQ Infra launched market engagement and consortium-selection activity for design and construction.
2025: Project materials described a tramway corridor of approximately 19 kilometers.
2025-2026: TramCité remained in development under CDPQ Infra leadership.

ownershipDetails: Firm: CDPQ | Fund: CDPQ Infrastructure | Ownership Interest: 30%
Firm: DigitalBridge | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Vertical Bridge owns and operates wireless towers and related communications infrastructure in the United States. Its customers are wireless carriers and other communications tenants that lease access to towers, rooftops, billboards, utility structures, and other sites. The business is asset-heavy and contracted because revenue depends on long-duration site leases and recurring tenancy agreements. Company materials describe Vertical Bridge as the largest private owner and operator of communications infrastructure in the United States, with more than 500,000 sites including more than 18,000 towers. Its principal operating footprint is in the United States. CDPQ acquired a 30% stake in April 2019, while the remaining ownership interests have not been publicly broken out in the materials reviewed.
historicalMilestones: 2014: Vertical Bridge was founded.
2019-04-02: CDPQ announced that it would acquire a 30% stake in Vertical Bridge.
2021-10-13: DigitalBridge announced completion of its acquisition of a controlling stake in Vertical Bridge.
2024-12-23: Vertical Bridge closed its previously announced transaction with Verizon for a tower portfolio.
2024-2026: Company materials described more than 500,000 sites nationwide, including more than 18,000 towers.

profileSubsector: Natural gas distribution and renewable gas
ownershipDetails: Firm: CDPQ | Fund: Trencap L.P. | Ownership Interest: 80.9%
Firm: Fonds de solidarité FTQ | Fund: Trencap L.P. | Ownership Interest: 19.1%
companyOverview: Énergir distributes natural gas and related energy services in Quebec and Vermont and is expanding activities in renewable natural gas, energy efficiency, and other low-carbon infrastructure. Its customer base includes residential, commercial, industrial, and institutional users served through regulated utility operations and related energy platforms. The business is asset-heavy and largely regulated because it depends on long-lived distribution networks, storage assets, and contracted infrastructure rather than merchant commodity exposure alone. Public company materials identify Quebec as the principal operating market, with an additional gas utility footprint in Vermont and renewable energy interests elsewhere in North America. CDPQ increased its stake in 2022 through Trencap L.P., after which the company disclosed ownership of 80.9% by CDPQ and 19.1% by Fonds de solidarité FTQ.
historicalMilestones: 1957: Company history materials identify the founding of Gaz Métropolitain, the predecessor to Énergir.
2017: Gaz Métro rebranded as Énergir as the group broadened its energy activities beyond traditional gas distribution.
2021-01-29: CDPQ and Fonds de solidarité FTQ announced an agreement to acquire additional shares in the business through Trencap.
2022-01-18: CDPQ completed its increased investment, and the company disclosed an ownership structure of 80.9% for CDPQ and 19.1% for Fonds de solidarité FTQ.

ownershipDetails: Firm: CIM Group | Fund: CIM Group / 1547 CSR JV | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
Firm: fifteenfortyseven Critical Systems Realty | Fund: CIM Group / 1547 CSR JV | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: 400 Paul Avenue Data Center is a Bay Area data-center campus being developed on a 7.3-acre site in San Francisco. The project is intended to serve hyperscale, cloud, and enterprise customers that require large blocks of powered shell or wholesale data-center capacity in a supply-constrained market. Its operating model is asset-heavy because value is tied to land, power infrastructure, and purpose-built data-center space rather than an asset-light services platform. Public project materials describe a planned campus of roughly 240,000 square feet, including a purpose-built data center and renovated support buildings, with marketing materials later referencing approximately 24 MW of capacity. CIM Group and fifteenfortyseven Critical Systems Realty announced the development partnership in 2020, and public sources do not disclose the partners' ownership percentages.
historicalMilestones: 2020-10-27: CIM Group and fifteenfortyseven Critical Systems Realty announced the start of construction on the 400 Paul Avenue data-center campus in San Francisco.
2020: The sponsors described a planned campus of approximately 240,000 square feet, including a purpose-built data center and renovated support buildings.
2022: Marketing materials for the campus referenced approximately 24 MW of powered-shell capacity for lease.
2024-2026: The project continued to be marketed in public data-center directories as a Bay Area wholesale data-center campus.

ownershipDetails: Firm: CIM Group | Fund: CIM Infrastructure Platform | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Antelope Valley Water Bank is a groundwater-storage asset in California that supports water banking and managed aquifer recharge. The asset is positioned to serve municipal and regional water agencies that need drought resilience and flexible storage capacity rather than conventional retail utility service. Its model is asset-heavy because value depends on land, water infrastructure, storage rights, and the ability to recharge and recover significant volumes over time. CIM has stated that it began investing in the project in 2007 and has described the site as spanning 1,838 acres with designed storage capacity of up to 500,000 acre-feet. Public sources reviewed do not disclose the current ownership structure beyond CIM's sponsorship of the asset.
historicalMilestones: 2007: CIM stated that it began investing in the Antelope Valley water bank.
2007-2022: Public descriptions of the asset identified groundwater storage design capacity of up to 500,000 acre-feet on approximately 1,838 acres.
2022: CIM highlighted the asset as part of its infrastructure strategy focused on water management and drought resilience in California.
2024: Public water-infrastructure materials continued to reference the Antelope Valley water-bank storage capacity in regional supply planning discussions.

ownershipDetails: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Bolder Industries converts end-of-life tires into recovered carbon black, petrochemical products, steel, and related outputs for industrial customers. Its end markets include tire manufacturing, rubber and plastics production, and other users seeking lower-carbon feedstocks and circular-economy inputs. The operating model is asset-heavy because performance depends on proprietary processing facilities, feedstock supply, and long-term offtake relationships rather than an asset-light services platform. Public materials point to operating activity in Missouri and expansion activity in Indiana, with additional international development activity disclosed by the company. CIM Group expanded its investment in 2021, Tiger Infrastructure announced a major growth investment in 2025, and the current ownership structure has not been publicly disclosed.
historicalMilestones: 2011: Public company profiles identify 2011 as the founding year.
2021-10-12: CIM Group announced that it had expanded its investment in Bolder Industries.
2022-09-01: Bolder announced a long-term end-of-life tire supply agreement with Liberty Tire Recycling to support feedstock for growth.
2025-02-11: Tiger Infrastructure Partners announced a transformational growth investment in Bolder Industries.

ownershipDetails: Firm: CIM Group | Fund: CIM Infrastructure Platform | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Kelso Valley Wind, Solar & Water is a CIM-sponsored California platform that combines renewable-energy development with land and water infrastructure. Public disclosures indicate the platform is intended to serve utility and community-choice offtake markets through solar generation while also managing supporting land and water assets. Its model is asset-heavy because the strategy depends on control of large land positions, renewable-energy projects, and related irrigation and water-conveyance infrastructure. Public reporting has stated that CIM acquired roughly 68,000 acres in Kern County in 2008 and later contracted initial generation with California public-power and community-choice counterparties. Public sources reviewed do not disclose the current ownership percentages beyond CIM's sponsorship of the platform.
historicalMilestones: 2008: Public reporting states that CIM acquired approximately 68,000 acres in Kern County that became the Kelso Valley platform.
2008-2021: CIM improved irrigation and water-conveyance systems on the property as part of the platform build-out.
2021: Public reporting said initial solar output from the platform had been contracted with Valley Clean Energy and Silicon Valley Power.
2021: CIM fund materials listed Kelso Valley among infrastructure investments within the platform.

ownershipDetails: Firm: CIM Group | Fund: CIM Infrastructure Platform | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Novva Data Centers develops and operates wholesale and colocation data centers across the western United States. Its customers include hyperscale, cloud, and enterprise users that require large-scale power, cooling, and connectivity for critical workloads. The business is asset-heavy because it depends on purpose-built campuses and significant electrical and mechanical infrastructure rather than a services-only model. Company materials describe an operating footprint spanning Utah, Colorado, Nevada, California, and Arizona, with growth centered on large, sustainability-oriented campuses. CIM Group provided a $355 million growth-equity investment in 2022 and, with Novva, announced $2 billion of new financing in 2025 to accelerate expansion, while public sources do not disclose the current ownership percentages.
historicalMilestones: 2020: Public materials identify 2020 as the launch year for Novva Data Centers.
2022-06-01: Novva announced a $355 million growth-equity investment from CIM Group.
2024-2025: Novva continued expanding its western U.S. campus footprint beyond Utah into Colorado, Nevada, California, and Arizona.
2025-03-05: CIM Group and Novva announced $2 billion of new financing to accelerate development across the western United States.

ownershipDetails: Firm: CIM Group | Fund: CIM Group / LAZ Parking JV | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
Firm: LAZ Parking Realty Investors | Fund: CIM Group / LAZ Parking JV | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Pine Street Garage is a downtown Seattle parking asset that serves commuters, visitors, and nearby retail and commercial uses. Revenue is generated through parking operations and related tenant activity, making the asset an asset-heavy transportation investment tied to a specific urban location. CIM's acquisition materials describe the property as an 11-level garage with 844 stalls and approximately 20,300 square feet of ground-floor retail. Its operating footprint is concentrated in Seattle's central business district, where access, location, and utilization drive performance. CIM acquired the asset in 2018 with partner LAZ Parking Realty Investors, and public sources do not disclose the partners' ownership percentages.
historicalMilestones: 2018-02-12: CIM Group announced the acquisition of Pine Street Garage in downtown Seattle.
2018: Acquisition materials described the property as an 11-level, 844-stall garage with approximately 20,300 square feet of ground-floor retail.
2018: CIM disclosed that the transaction was completed with partner LAZ Parking Realty Investors.
2018-2026: The asset continued to operate as a private urban parking facility within CIM's Seattle portfolio.

ownershipDetails: Firm: CIM Group | Fund: CIM Group / LAZ Parking JV | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
Firm: LAZ Parking Realty Investors | Fund: CIM Group / LAZ Parking JV | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Sixth Avenue Garage is a downtown Seattle parking structure located at the base of the Hilton Downtown Seattle. The asset serves hotel guests, office users, and other downtown drivers through location-based parking demand rather than a broad transportation-network model. Its economics are asset-heavy because returns depend on the garage structure, stall count, operating management, and surrounding central-business-district demand. CIM described the asset as a 520-stall, 11-level parking structure when it announced the acquisition. CIM acquired the property through a joint venture with LAZ Parking Realty Investors in late 2019 and publicly announced the transaction in January 2020, while the ownership split was not disclosed.
historicalMilestones: 2019-12-24: Transaction counsel announced CIM's acquisition of Sixth Avenue Garage through a joint venture with LAZ Parking Realty Investors.
2020-01-13: CIM publicly announced the acquisition of the 520-stall parking structure in downtown Seattle.
2020: CIM stated that the asset sits at the base of the Hilton Downtown Seattle and expanded its Seattle transportation footprint.

ownershipDetails: Firm: CIM Group | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
Firm: Abundant Solar Power Inc. / SolarBank Corporation | Fund: New HoldCo | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: SolarBank Joint Venture (New HoldCo) is a newly formed vehicle created to own and finance a portfolio of U.S. solar assets associated with SolarBank. The underlying projects serve renewable-power end markets, including community-solar and distributed-generation applications, through long-lived operating assets rather than an asset-light development advisory model. Public transaction materials describe a portfolio of 97 MW of identified U.S. renewable-energy assets to be funded through the structure. The model is asset-heavy and project-based because the vehicle is designed to acquire project interests and support construction and ownership of operating renewable infrastructure. SolarBank announced in May 2025 that CIM would provide up to $100 million of project-based financing and preferred equity through the newly formed joint-venture structure, while the ownership percentages were not publicly disclosed.
historicalMilestones: 2025-05-06: SolarBank and CIM announced a mandate for up to $100 million of financing for 97 MW of U.S. renewable-energy assets.

2025: SolarBank disclosed that CIM would acquire preferred equity interests in New HoldCo as part of the financing structure.
2025-2026: The vehicle was positioned to acquire identified U.S. project interests from the SolarBank development portfolio.

ownershipDetails: Firm: CIM Group | Fund: CIM Infrastructure Fund III | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Terreva Renewables develops, owns, and operates renewable natural gas projects that capture methane and upgrade it for pipeline injection and related end uses. Its customers and counterparties include gas utilities, pipeline-connected offtakers, and other users of contracted RNG volumes and environmental attributes. The business is asset-heavy because performance depends on landfill-gas capture systems, upgrading facilities, and long-lived project infrastructure. Public materials describe a North American RNG platform that CIM founded in 2022 through the acquisition of 100% of MAS CanAm's development platform and later expanded with additional capital. CIM announced in 2023 that Terreva had secured $417 million of new investment for growth, while public sources do not disclose the current ownership percentages.
historicalMilestones: 2022: CIM founded Terreva as a portfolio company through the acquisition of 100% of MAS CanAm, LLC's RNG development platform.
2022: The founding acquisition included a portfolio of in-construction and development RNG assets.

2024: CIM case-study materials highlighted continued expansion of the RNG platform across North America.

ownershipDetails: Firm: CIM Group | Fund: CIM Infrastructure Platform | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Westlands Electric Power Company is a CIM-sponsored power platform formed to hold California renewable-generation assets. The platform is intended to sell electricity and related products into utility and long-term contracted power markets rather than operate as a regulated retail utility. Its model is asset-heavy because value is tied to owned solar and storage facilities, interconnection rights, and project-level contracting. CIM stated in 2025 that the initial WEPCO portfolio consisted of seven solar-generation facilities and one battery-storage facility located in Westlands Solar Park. CIM formed the company in 2025 from CIM-affiliated entities, and public sources do not disclose the ownership percentages of the current investor group.
historicalMilestones: 2020-03-02: CIM announced the start of construction at Westlands Solar Park, the asset base that would later support WEPCO.
2025-10-13: CIM announced the formation of Westlands Electric Power Company.
2025: CIM said WEPCO's initial portfolio included seven solar facilities and one battery-storage facility in Westlands Solar Park.
2025-2026: CIM stated that WEPCO was intended to grow through both organic development and additional acquisitions.

ownershipDetails: Firm: CIM Group | Fund: CIM Infrastructure Platform | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Westlands Solar Park is a large California renewable-energy development centered on utility-scale solar generation and storage. The project is designed to serve utilities and other power purchasers through long-term offtake arrangements rather than merchant exposure alone. Its operating model is asset-heavy because the value proposition depends on control of land, interconnection, generation assets, and associated storage infrastructure. CIM has described Westlands Solar Park as one of the largest permitted solar parks in North America, with total planned capacity that can exceed 2,700 MW at full build-out. Public sources identify Aquamarine Solar as part of the development, and CIM continues to sponsor the broader platform through privately held entities.
historicalMilestones: 2020-03-02: CIM announced the start of construction at Westlands Solar Park.
2020: CIM stated that the permitted development could exceed 2,700 MW of total capacity at full build-out.
2021: Public reporting referenced the first phase of the solar park and associated offtake arrangements with California public-power buyers.
2025: CIM stated that the initial WEPCO portfolio at Westlands Solar Park included seven solar facilities and one battery-storage facility.

companyOverview: 407 ETR operates an all-electronic toll highway across the Greater Toronto Area. Its customers are passenger and commercial vehicle users seeking time savings on a major east-west corridor, and revenue is generated from tolling and related roadway services. The business is asset-heavy and concession-based because performance depends on a long-life road network, traffic volumes, toll policy, and maintenance of the concession asset. Company disclosures state that the highway extends 108 kilometres and that 407 International became the owner of the concession company in 1999. Public ownership disclosures show that, following 2025 transactions, Ferrovial owns 48.29%, CPP Investments and other institutional investors own 44.20%, and PSP Investments owns 7.51%.
historicalMilestones: 1999: 407 International became the owner of the 407 ETR Concession Company.

profileSubsector: Electric, gas, and water utility holding company
ownershipDetails: Firm: CPP Investments | Fund: Real Assets (Infrastructure) | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
Firm: Global Infrastructure Partners | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: ALLETE is a regulated utility holding company whose businesses include Minnesota Power, Superior Water, Light and Power, ALLETE Clean Energy, BNI Energy, and New Energy Equity. Its customer base includes regulated electric, gas, and water users as well as renewable-energy and energy-infrastructure counterparties across several subsidiaries. The model is asset-heavy and predominantly regulated because earnings are supported by utility rate bases, generation and transmission assets, and other long-life infrastructure. The company's 2024 annual report stated that it employed 1,616 people at year-end 2024 and remained headquartered in Duluth, Minnesota. ALLETE completed its acquisition by CPP Investments and Global Infrastructure Partners in December 2025, and public sources reviewed do not disclose the ownership split between those two investors.
historicalMilestones: 2024-05-06: ALLETE announced an agreement to be acquired by a partnership led by CPP Investments and Global Infrastructure Partners.

ownershipDetails: Firm: ArcLight Capital Partners | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
Firm: CPP Investments | Fund: Sustainable Energies | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: AlphaGen owns and operates a large U.S. independent power portfolio focused on electricity generation assets. Its customers are wholesale power markets, load-serving entities, and other counterparties that rely on dispatchable generation and capacity from established plants. The platform is asset-heavy because it depends on owned generation assets and plant operations rather than a fee-based services model. CPP Investments stated in 2025 that AlphaGen is one of the largest independent power portfolios in the United States with more than 11 GW of capacity. ArcLight announced a US$1.0 billion investment from CPP Investments in 2025, and public sources do not disclose the ownership percentages of the current investor group.

companyOverview: Caturus Energy is an integrated natural gas and LNG platform focused on low-emissions gas supply and related infrastructure. The business serves LNG and gas markets through upstream production and associated commercialization rather than a regulated utility model. Its economics are asset-heavy because value depends on acreage, production, processing, and related physical infrastructure. Company materials describe a position of more than 200,000 net acres in Texas and a strategy tied to supplying lower-emissions natural gas into growing LNG demand. CPP Investments reported in 2025 that it held a 12% stake, while broader ownership details have not been publicly disclosed.
historicalMilestones: 2025: CPP Investments disclosed a 12% stake in Caturus Energy in its quarterly results materials.
2025: Company materials described a strategy focused on integrated natural gas and LNG with more than 200,000 net acres in Texas.

ownershipDetails: Firm: Pattern Energy Group LP | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Cordelio Power develops, builds, and operates wind, solar, and energy-storage projects in the United States and Canada. Its customers are utilities, corporates, and other power offtakers that contract for renewable generation and related capacity. The business is asset-heavy and largely contracted because value is linked to owned and under-construction projects plus a sizable development pipeline. Public company materials have described an operating portfolio of 1,860 MW across the United States and Canada and a U.S. development pipeline of more than 18,000 MW. Pattern Energy completed its acquisition of Cordelio in April 2026 after the platform had been wholly owned by CPP Investments, and the direct ownership percentages of the resulting structure have not been publicly disclosed.

Firm: Enbridge Inc. | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: 51%
companyOverview: Enbridge Onshore Renewables JV is a North American renewable-power partnership holding select onshore wind and solar interests. The platform serves wholesale electricity and contracted offtake markets through long-life generating assets rather than a merchant development-only model. Its economics are asset-heavy because returns are tied to operating projects, contracted revenues, and ongoing asset management. CPP Investments announced in 2018 that it would acquire a 49% interest in Enbridge's interests in select North American onshore renewable assets, leaving Enbridge with 51%. Public disclosures indicate that the venture spans both Canada and the United States, with the current ownership split publicly disclosed at 49% for CPP Investments and 51% for Enbridge.

companyOverview: Equinix xScale U.S. JV is a hyperscale data-center partnership focused on developing very large U.S. campuses for cloud and AI workloads. Its customers are the largest cloud and hyperscale users that need core deployment capacity adjacent to Equinix's interconnection ecosystem. The venture is asset-heavy because it is designed to acquire land, construct greater-than-100 MW campuses, and finance long-life digital infrastructure. Equinix announced in October 2024 that the partners intended to raise more than US$15 billion over time and eventually add more than 1.5 GW of new U.S. capacity. Under the disclosed terms, CPP Investments and GIC each control 37.5% of the venture and Equinix owns 25%.

ownershipDetails: Firm: Ocean Winds | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: 50%
Firm: CPP Investments | Fund: Sustainable Energies | Ownership Interest: 50%
companyOverview: Golden State Wind is a California offshore-wind development platform created by Ocean Winds and CPP Investments. The venture targets large-scale power supply into California's future renewable-energy market through long-dated offshore wind development rather than an operating utility model. Its business model is asset-heavy and developmental because value depends on seabed lease rights, permitting, transmission planning, and future project execution. CPP Investments disclosed that Golden State Wind is a 50-50 joint venture and that it won a federal lease area capable of supporting roughly 2 GW of offshore wind generation off California's Central Coast. Public sources do not disclose additional ownership tiers beyond the equal partnership between Ocean Winds and CPP Investments.
historicalMilestones: 2023-01-18: CPP Investments and Ocean Winds announced that Golden State Wind had won a California offshore-wind lease area.
2023: The partners disclosed that the lease could support approximately 2 GW of offshore wind capacity.
2023: The venture committed US$30 million to workforce development and community benefit initiatives tied to the lease award.
2023-2026: Golden State Wind continued advancing California offshore-wind development planning.

principalLocationOfOperations: Multiple U.S. states and Canadian provinces
ownershipDetails: Firm: CPP Investments | Fund: Sustainable Energies | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
Firm: APG Asset Management / ABP | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
Firm: Australian Retirement Trust | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
Firm: Pattern management | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Pattern Energy develops and operates renewable generation and transmission infrastructure across North America. Its customers are utilities, corporates, and power purchasers that contract for wind, solar, storage, and transmission capacity over long durations. The platform is asset-heavy and largely contracted because it owns and develops physical infrastructure rather than providing advisory or trading services alone. Company materials in 2026 described more than 40 facilities across North America and nearly 12,000 MW of operating and in-construction capacity following the Cordelio acquisition. CPP Investments acquired Pattern in 2020, APG and Australian Retirement Trust closed on Riverstone's stake in 2025, and public sources identify CPP Investments as the majority shareholder while not disclosing individual current ownership percentages.
historicalMilestones: 2009: Public company materials identify 2009 as the founding year for Pattern Energy.
2020-03-16: CPP Investments completed the take-private acquisition of Pattern Energy.
2025-06-09: Pattern announced the closing of an equity investment from a consortium headed by APG and Australian Retirement Trust, which acquired Riverstone's stake.
2026-04-02: Pattern completed the acquisition of Cordelio Power, expanding the platform across the United States and Canada.

oneSentenceDescription: Ports America is the largest independent marine terminal operator in the United States, providing stevedoring, terminal management, and related port services at approximately 70 port locations across both coasts and the Gulf.
ownershipDetails: Firm: CPP Investments | Fund: Real Assets (Infrastructure) | Ownership Interest: 100%
companyOverview: Ports America operates marine terminals and provides stevedoring, cargo handling, cruise, auto, and related port services across the United States. Its customers include ocean carriers, port authorities, cruise lines, auto shippers, and cargo owners that require terminal access and operating expertise at critical logistics nodes. The business is asset-heavy and concession-oriented because it relies on terminal leases, port equipment, and operating agreements across major coastal gateways. Public transaction materials describe approximately 70 locations in 33 ports and annual throughput that includes containers, general cargo, vehicles, and cruise passengers. CPP Investments announced in 2021 that it would become the 100% owner of Ports America and later reported in 2022 that it had expanded its investment to full ownership.
historicalMilestones: 2005: Ports America was formed through the combination of predecessor terminal and stevedoring businesses.
2014-2015: CPP Investments became a minority investor in Ports America.
2021-09-29: CPP Investments announced a definitive agreement to acquire Oaktree's interest and become the 100% owner of Ports America.
2022-12-15: CPP Investments reported that it had expanded its investment in Ports America to full ownership.

ownershipDetails: Firm: CPP Investments | Fund: Sustainable Energies | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Redaptive provides energy-as-a-service financing and execution for building-efficiency, renewable-power, and related decarbonization projects. Its customers are enterprise and institutional building owners that seek lower energy use and infrastructure upgrades without funding the full upfront capital cost themselves. The model is asset-heavy from a financing perspective because the company funds and manages installed equipment portfolios across customer sites under long-term contractual arrangements. Public materials describe solutions spanning energy efficiency, project finance, and equipment finance, with CPP Investments first investing in 2022 and increasing that investment in 2024. Public sources reviewed do not disclose the current ownership percentages of Redaptive's investor group.
historicalMilestones: 2022-12-19: CPP Investments announced an approximately US$200 million investment in Redaptive.
2024-10-22: Redaptive announced an additional US$100 million equity investment from CPP Investments.
2025-05-08: Redaptive announced a US$650 million credit facility from CDPQ and Nuveen to support platform expansion.
2025-2026: The company continued expanding its energy-as-a-service offering across efficiency and renewable infrastructure.

oneSentenceDescription: Sempra Infrastructure Partners is a subsidiary of Sempra (NYSE: SRE) that develops, builds, and operates large-scale LNG export, natural gas pipelines, and renewable energy infrastructure in North America.
ownershipDetails: Firm: Sempra | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
Firm: CPP Investments | Fund: Real Assets (Infrastructure) | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Sempra Infrastructure develops, owns, and operates LNG, gas, and related energy infrastructure in North America. Its customer base includes LNG offtakers, gas-market participants, and energy counterparties that rely on large-scale export, storage, and transportation assets. The platform is asset-heavy and largely contracted because its economics are tied to long-life LNG and pipeline infrastructure rather than asset-light services. Public materials identify key assets and projects including Cameron LNG in Louisiana and Port Arthur LNG in Texas, with major capital commitments underway on the Gulf Coast. CPP Investments acquired a 20% equity interest in Sempra Infrastructure Partners in 2021, while broader current ownership percentages beyond Sempra and CPP Investments were not disclosed in the sources reviewed.
historicalMilestones: 2021: Sempra Infrastructure Partners was formed to house Sempra's LNG, energy-networks, and related infrastructure businesses.
2021-09-28: CPP Investments announced the acquisition of a 20% equity interest in Sempra Infrastructure Partners.
2023-03: Port Arthur LNG Phase 1 reached final investment decision.
2024-2026: Construction continued on Port Arthur LNG Phase 1 and related Gulf Coast infrastructure.

principalLocationOfOperations: Colorado; Kansas; Nebraska; Wyoming; multiple U.S. states
ownershipDetails: Firm: Blackstone Infrastructure Partners | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
Firm: CPP Investments | Fund: Sustainable Energies | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Tallgrass Energy owns and operates U.S. midstream infrastructure spanning natural gas pipelines, crude pipelines, terminals, and emerging low-carbon transport initiatives. Its customers include producers, utilities, refiners, marketers, and industrial users that depend on long-haul pipeline and storage infrastructure. The platform is asset-heavy and largely contracted because cash flow is tied to pipeline systems, terminal assets, and transportation agreements rather than fee-light advisory activity. Public company materials before privatization described approximately 8,400 miles of natural gas pipelines and 800 miles of crude pipelines, and CPP Investments said in 2024 that Tallgrass has assets across 14 states. CPP Investments announced in 2024 that it had acquired a stake in the business in partnership with Blackstone, while the ownership percentages were not publicly disclosed.
historicalMilestones: 2012: Tallgrass Energy was formed and listed its initial midstream assets in the public markets.
2019-03-11: Blackstone announced closing of its purchase of a controlling interest in Tallgrass Energy.
2020-02: Tallgrass completed its take-private transaction under Blackstone sponsorship.
2024-08-13: CPP Investments announced that it had acquired a stake in Tallgrass Energy in partnership with Blackstone.

ownershipDetails: Firm: CPP Investments | Fund: Real Assets (Infrastructure) | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
Firm: funds managed by Blue Owl Capital | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
Firm: Crusoe / Primary Digital Infrastructure | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Texas Abilene Hyperscale Data Centre refers to the large-scale AI and cloud data-center development underway in Abilene, Texas. The project is designed for hyperscale customers and depends on substantial land, power, and construction commitments, making it an asset-heavy digital-infrastructure investment. Public disclosures around the Abilene campus describe an initial 206 MW phase and a broader multi-building development plan that scales materially beyond that first phase. CPP Investments stated in 2025 that it invested in the second phase of the project alongside funds managed by Blue Owl, while other public materials identify Crusoe and Primary Digital Infrastructure as key development partners. Public sources reviewed do not disclose the current ownership percentages across the project consortium.
historicalMilestones: 2024-10-15: Crusoe, Blue Owl Capital, and Primary Digital Infrastructure announced an initial JV-backed hyperscale data-center development in Abilene.
2025-05-21: Crusoe, Blue Owl, and Primary Digital announced the second phase of the Abilene development program.
2025: CPP Investments reported an investment in the second phase of the Abilene hyperscale data-center project.
2026-03-27: Crusoe announced an additional 900 MW AI factory campus in Abilene adjacent to the earlier development.

ownershipDetails: Firm: CPP Investments | Fund: Real Assets (Infrastructure) | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
Firm: Brookfield | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: TransAlta Keephills Data Centre JV is a development partnership for a large Alberta data-center project linked to dedicated power supply at the Keephills site. The venture is designed to serve high-density computing demand and depends on both digital-infrastructure development and long-term power procurement. Its model is asset-heavy because value is tied to site control, major capital deployment, and integrated electricity infrastructure rather than an asset-light colocation strategy. TransAlta announced in 2026 that it had entered into an MOU with CPP Investments and Brookfield for an initial approximately 230 MW project with potential to scale to 1 GW. Public sources reviewed do not disclose the ownership percentages of the development partners.
historicalMilestones: 2026-02-27: TransAlta announced an MOU with CPP Investments and Brookfield for data-center development at its Keephills site.
2026: The partners disclosed an initial approximately 230 MW project supported by a long-term power arrangement.
2026: The project was described as having the potential to scale to as much as 1 GW over time.
2026-2027: The development advanced as a powered data-center opportunity in Alberta.

ownershipDetails: Firm: Transurban | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: 50%

companyOverview: Transurban Chesapeake owns the 495, 95, and 395 Express Lanes system serving the greater Washington, D.C. region. Its customers are passenger and commercial motorists that pay variable tolls for managed-lane access and travel-time reliability. The platform is asset-heavy and concession-based because value depends on long-dated toll-road rights, lane assets, traffic demand, and ongoing operations. CPP Investments disclosed in 2020 that it acquired a 15% interest, and subsequent Transurban investor materials show a current ownership split of 50% Transurban, 25% AustralianSuper, 15% CPP Investments, and 10% UniSuper. The asset base remains a core managed-lanes platform in Virginia, Maryland, and the District of Columbia.
historicalMilestones: 2020-12-16: CPP Investments announced the acquisition of a 15% interest in Transurban Chesapeake.
2020-12-17: Transurban disclosed the broader Chesapeake partnership ownership structure and governance framework.

2025-2026: Transurban investor materials continued to show ownership of 50% Transurban, 25% AustralianSuper, 15% CPP Investments, and 10% UniSuper.

profileSubsector: Mobile power generation and energy optimization
ownershipDetails: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: VoltaGrid provides mobile power generation, energy optimization, and related infrastructure services for customers that need temporary, distributed, or flexible power solutions. Its end markets include oil and gas, utilities, data-center and industrial users, and other customers requiring reliable off-grid or behind-the-meter electricity. The business is asset-heavy because it owns and deploys physical generation equipment and related field infrastructure. Public financing disclosures show an investor group that has included Carnelian Energy Capital, Pilot Company, Walter Ventures, Longbow Capital, and CPP Investments, while CPP also provided a term loan in 2024. The current ownership structure has not been publicly disclosed.
historicalMilestones: 2020-12: VoltaGrid announced a US$73 million equity commitment from institutional investors including CPP Investments and other energy-focused backers.

2024-2026: VoltaGrid continued expanding its mobile and distributed power platform in the United States.

companyOverview: Williams Ohio Valley Midstream JV is a natural-gas midstream partnership built around gathering, processing, and takeaway infrastructure in the Appalachian basin. Its customers are upstream producers and other gas-market participants that require dependable midstream capacity in the Utica and surrounding areas. The model is asset-heavy and fee-based because returns depend on pipelines, processing facilities, and long-term throughput arrangements rather than trading activity. Public transaction materials state that CPP Investments invested approximately US$1.34 billion in 2019 for a 35% interest and that Williams retained 65%. The joint venture combines Ohio Valley Midstream and Utica East Ohio Midstream assets serving Ohio and West Virginia.
historicalMilestones: 2019-01-14: Williams and CPP Investments announced the formation of a new Appalachian midstream joint venture.
2019: CPP Investments committed approximately US$1.34 billion for a 35% interest, with Williams retaining 65%.
2019: The transaction combined Ohio Valley Midstream and Utica East Ohio Midstream assets.
2019-2026: The joint venture continued operating gathering and processing infrastructure across Ohio and West Virginia.

companyOverview: Wolf Midstream develops and operates energy-infrastructure systems in Alberta, including natural gas liquids and carbon-related infrastructure. Its customers include industrial and energy-market counterparties that require processing, transportation, and associated midstream services. The platform is asset-heavy because it depends on physical pipeline and plant infrastructure with long useful lives and contracted or quasi-contracted throughput. CPP Investments partnered with Wolf in 2015 to form the business and has since highlighted the platform as a long-term Alberta infrastructure investment. Public sources reviewed do not disclose the current ownership percentages between CPP Investments and Wolf's management or sponsor entities.

ownershipDetails: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: American Student Transportation Partners provides outsourced school transportation services for K-12 districts in the United States. The platform serves school districts through route-based, contracted operations using a large fleet of buses and vans rather than passenger-demand or asset-light logistics models. Its business is asset-heavy because service delivery depends on owned or controlled vehicles, depots, drivers, and local operating systems. CVC DIF announced in 2025 that ASTP transported more than 90,000 students each school year across the Northeast and Midwest through a fleet of more than 2,300 vehicles and contracts with over 50 districts. CVC DIF also stated that it had agreed to acquire ASTP from Access Holdings through DIF Infrastructure VII, and public sources reviewed do not disclose the final post-closing ownership structure.
historicalMilestones: 2021: Public sources identify ASTP as having been established in 2021.
2021-2025: ASTP expanded into a multi-state student transportation provider serving the Northeast and Midwest.
2025-08-12: CVC DIF announced an agreement to acquire ASTP from Access Holdings through DIF Infrastructure VII.
2025: CVC DIF disclosed that ASTP served more than 90,000 students annually with a fleet of more than 2,300 vehicles.

ownershipDetails: Firm: CVC DIF | Fund: DIF CIF III | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Airtower Networks designs, builds, owns, and operates in-building wireless and related digital-infrastructure systems. Its customers include property owners, enterprises, and wireless-network stakeholders that need neutral-host connectivity, distributed antenna systems, public-safety communications, and related managed services. The platform is asset-heavy because it deploys and manages physical network infrastructure across customer sites rather than operating as a software-only services business. DIF announced in 2022 that it would become Airtower's growth-capital partner through CIF III to support a nationwide expansion strategy. Public sources reviewed do not disclose the resulting ownership percentages or the full current cap table.

ownershipDetails: Firm: CVC DIF | Fund: DIF Infrastructure VI | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Bernhard provides engineering, construction, and energy-as-a-service solutions for building infrastructure in sectors such as healthcare, education, government, and commercial real estate. Its customers rely on the company for design, modernization, and long-term performance of mechanical, electrical, and energy systems rather than discrete equipment supply alone. The platform combines services and asset-backed project delivery, with public materials highlighting more than 2,000 employees, 23 office locations, and over 2,500 active projects. Bernhard Capital Partners agreed to sell the business to DIF Infrastructure VI in 2021, and Bernhard announced a rebrand to ENFRA in 2025. Public sources reviewed do not disclose DIF's ownership percentage.

companyOverview: BluEarth Renewables develops, builds, owns, and operates renewable-power assets across Canada and the United States. Its customers are utilities and other power purchasers that contract for wind, hydro, and solar output under long-term arrangements. The business is asset-heavy and largely contracted because its value is anchored in operating projects, construction assets, and a large development pipeline. Company materials describe more than 750 MWAC gross in operation, under construction, or with signed PPAs, along with a development pipeline exceeding 7 GW. BluEarth announced in 2019 that DIF Infrastructure V had closed on the acquisition of 100% of the company.

companyOverview: Brahms Wind is an onshore wind project in New Mexico. Its output is sold into electricity markets and contracted renewable-power arrangements through a single-asset operating model rather than a diversified service platform. The business is asset-heavy because value depends on turbines, land rights, and long-term operations of a physical generation asset. Public project materials describe Brahms as a 20 MW project in Curry County, New Mexico, and indicate that BayWa r.e. developed the asset before DIF acquired it through a broader 2019 U.S. wind-portfolio transaction. Public sources reviewed do not disclose DIF's exact ownership percentage for the project.

companyOverview: CARMA provides submetering, utility billing, and essential building services for multi-residential and commercial properties in Canada. Its customers are building owners, managers, and residents that require consumption measurement, billing administration, and related services tied to utility infrastructure. The platform is infrastructure-linked and recurring because it depends on installed metering assets, contracted service relationships, and long-duration building exposure. CVC DIF announced in 2025 that CARMA operated across more than 1,000 buildings and approximately 135,000 units in Ontario, Alberta, British Columbia, and Nova Scotia. CVC DIF also stated that it had signed a definitive agreement to acquire CARMA through DIF Infrastructure VIII, and public sources reviewed do not disclose the post-closing ownership structure.

companyOverview: Cascade Power Project is a large combined-cycle gas-fired generation facility in Alberta. The asset serves the Alberta electricity market through utility-scale generation capacity rather than a regulated retail utility model. Its economics are asset-heavy because they depend on a major power plant, long-term operations, fuel supply, and dispatch performance. Public project materials describe the facility as approximately 900 MW to 932 MW and state that it can supply a meaningful share of Alberta's electricity demand. DIF announced in 2020 that it was investing in the project through DIF Infrastructure VI alongside Axium Infrastructure and OPTrust, while exact ownership percentages were not publicly disclosed.

companyOverview: Diverso Energy develops, owns, and operates geothermal heating and cooling systems for multi-unit residential and commercial buildings in Canada. Its customers are building developers and owners that procure low-carbon heating and cooling under energy-as-a-service contracts rather than owning the full infrastructure themselves. The platform is asset-heavy and contracted because returns are supported by installed ground-source systems and long-term service agreements. DIF announced in 2023 that it would acquire a 75% interest through DIF Infrastructure VII and that the founders would retain 25%. The asset serves the William Osler Health System and the surrounding community through availability-based hospital infrastructure rather than commercial user-pay revenue. Its model is asset-heavy and contract-based because value depends on the construction, financing, and long-term delivery of a major clinical facility. Public project materials describe a new four-storey tower adding about 250,000 square feet to the hospital and housing critical emergency, intensive care, neonatal, and ambulatory services. The platform is asset-heavy because it develops and owns physical solar assets rather than operating solely as a services or brokerage business. DIF announced in 2023 that it had agreed to acquire a majority equity interest in the company through DIF Infrastructure VII, while public sources do not disclose the final ownership percentage.

companyOverview: Idaho Wind Partners is an operating onshore wind portfolio near Twin Falls, Idaho. The asset serves contracted renewable-power markets through a long-lived generation portfolio rather than a development-only model. Its business is asset-heavy because value depends on turbines, site rights, transmission access, and long-term operations. DIF acquired the project as part of a broader U.S. wind-portfolio transaction in 2019, and public sources do not disclose DIF's exact ownership percentage.

companyOverview: JW Water Holdings and the related Robson utilities form a regulated water and wastewater platform in Arizona. companyOverview: Joink provides fiber, fixed-wireless, voice, hosting, and managed technology services in the Midwest. Company materials describe a footprint across eastern Illinois and western Indiana and trace the business from its Terre Haute origins into a broader regional ISP platform. companyOverview: SBA Canada owns and manages wireless-tower infrastructure in Canada. Its customers are mobile-network operators and other communications counterparties that require leased vertical real estate under long-term agreements. The business is asset-heavy because value depends on tower sites, ground rights, and long-duration tenancy contracts rather than asset-light telecom service provision. CVC DIF announced in 2025 that SBA Canada had been established in 2009 and comprised approximately 500 owned and managed sites across the country. CVC DIF also stated that it had agreed to acquire the platform through DIF Infrastructure VIII, while public sources reviewed do not disclose the final post-closing ownership structure.

ownershipDetails: Firm: CVC DIF | Fund: DIF CIF III | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.

companyOverview: Tonaquint Data Centers provides colocation, cloud, and related data-center services in the Mountain West and adjoining markets. Its customers include enterprise and channel users that require regional colocation capacity, managed infrastructure, and connectivity. The business is asset-heavy because it owns and operates data-center facilities rather than a software-only platform. DIF announced in 2023 that it had acquired Tonaquint through CIF III and that management would retain a minority stake, while later disclosures showed platform expansion into Oklahoma City through the EdgeX acquisition. Public sources identify Tonaquint as founded in 2008 and operating across Utah, Idaho, and Oklahoma.

ownershipDetails: Firm: CVC DIF | Fund: DIF CIF II | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Valley Fiber builds and operates rural broadband infrastructure in Manitoba. Its customers are households, businesses, and communities that need high-speed connectivity in underserved areas rather than dense urban telecom markets. The platform is asset-heavy because growth depends on fiber construction, last-mile network deployment, and long-term customer infrastructure rather than a resale-only model. Company and financing materials describe Valley Fiber as based in Winkler, Manitoba and focused on expanding dedicated fiber infrastructure across rural communities. Public transaction materials identify DIF as a partner in the Manitoba fibre project and a sponsor of the platform, while exact ownership percentages have not been publicly disclosed.
historicalMilestones: 2016-12: Valley Fiber stated that it was incorporated in December 2016.
2022: Valley Fiber completed the asset purchase of Simply Connected as part of platform expansion.

ownershipDetails: Firm: Carlyle Infrastructure | Fund: Carlyle Renewable & Sustainable Energy Fund | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
Firm: BNRG | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Acadia Renewable Energy develops utility-scale solar and battery-storage projects in the United States. Its customers are utilities, municipalities, and other power purchasers that contract for renewable generation and related capacity. The platform is asset-heavy because it depends on land control, permitting, interconnection, and project ownership rather than advisory work alone. Carlyle announced in 2020 that it and partner BNRG had acquired a large Maine solar-development portfolio through a newly established Acadia affiliate, and later company materials showed expansion into additional U.S. development opportunities including New York. Public sources reviewed do not disclose the ownership percentages between Carlyle and BNRG.

principalLocationOfOperations: Maryland; New York; multiple U.S. states
ownershipDetails: Firm: Carlyle Infrastructure | Fund: Carlyle Global Infrastructure Opportunity Fund | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
Firm: Schneider Electric | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: AlphaStruxure develops, finances, builds, owns, and operates microgrids and other on-site energy infrastructure under energy-as-a-service structures. Its customers are public and private institutions that need resilient, lower-carbon power systems without taking full ownership of the underlying infrastructure. The platform is asset-heavy and contracted because projects are financed and operated over long terms around physical generation, storage, and controls equipment. Carlyle and Schneider Electric launched the joint venture in 2019, and public materials highlight projects such as transit electrification infrastructure and the New Terminal One microgrid at JFK Airport. Public sources reviewed do not disclose the ownership percentages between Carlyle and Schneider Electric.

ownershipVehicle: Carlyle Global Infrastructure Opportunity Fund / Renewable & Sustainable Energy Fund
profileSubsector: Solar, storage, and renewable energy platform

ownershipDetails: Firm: Carlyle | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Amp Energy develops, owns, and operates renewable generation and storage projects across North America, Australia, and the United Kingdom. Its customers include utilities, corporate offtakers, community-solar participants, and electricity markets that procure renewable energy, capacity, and related services. The platform is asset-heavy because it develops, finances, constructs, and operates physical solar, storage, and related infrastructure assets rather than acting only as a service provider. Public company materials describe a multi-gigawatt portfolio and pipeline spanning utility-scale and distributed projects, including community-solar activity in New York. Carlyle announced a capital commitment to the company in 2021, while 2025 disclosures also showed a carve-out of certain Australia and United Kingdom assets into the separately branded Revera platform.

companyOverview: ARK Data Centers provides colocation, interconnection, and hybrid-cloud data-center services for enterprise and institutional customers. Its customer base includes businesses that require regional data-center capacity, cloud connectivity, and managed infrastructure across secondary United States markets. The platform is asset-heavy because it owns and operates data-center facilities and related infrastructure rather than offering software-only services. Company materials trace the business to 2007 and describe a footprint across seven United States markets. Carlyle announced the acquisition in December 2021, closed the transaction in February 2022, and the company rebranded from Involta to ARK Data Centers in 2024.

principalLocationOfOperations: New York; multiple United States states
ownershipDetails: Firm: Carlyle | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Aspen Power develops, acquires, finances, owns, and operates distributed solar and storage assets in the United States. Its customers include commercial users, municipalities, and community-solar subscribers that procure contracted distributed-generation capacity rather than owning the underlying assets directly. The platform is asset-heavy and largely contracted because it depends on owning and operating physical projects supported by long-term power contracts and subscription arrangements. Company materials state that Aspen launched in 2020 and, as of 2025, had financed more than $2.5 billion and supported more than 600 projects. Carlyle announced a $350 million investment in 2022 to support growth and acquisition activity, including Aspen's acquisition of Safari Energy.

oneSentenceDescription: CAG Holdings is the concessionaire consortium developing and operating the New Terminal One (NTO) at John F.
profileCompanyName: CAG Holdings / The New Terminal One at JFK
ownershipDetails: Firm: Carlyle | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
Firm: JLC Infrastructure | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
Firm: Ullico | Fund: Ullico Infrastructure Fund | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: CAG Holdings is the Carlyle-backed airport infrastructure platform associated with the New Terminal One redevelopment at John F. Kennedy International Airport. The project company develops, finances, constructs, and will operate a new international terminal serving airlines and passengers under a long-term lease and concession structure with the Port Authority of New York and New Jersey. The model is asset-heavy and contract-based because returns depend on terminal infrastructure, phased construction, and long-term airport operations rather than a light-asset services model. Public project materials describe the New Terminal One as a 2.4 million-square-foot facility with 23 gates and a total project value of about $9.5 billion. Carlyle established CAG Holdings in 2019 as a dedicated airport infrastructure platform, and the JFK consortium reached financial close and full notice to proceed in 2022.
historicalMilestones: 2019-11-21: Carlyle announced the launch of CAG Holdings as a dedicated United States airport infrastructure platform.

2023-06: Construction activity accelerated at the site as phased demolition and major works progressed.
2024: Public project updates reported continued structural and foundation progress ahead of the first phase opening.

ownershipDetails: Firm: Carlyle | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
Firm: Alchemy Renewable Energy | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Cardinal Renewables is a solar platform formed by Carlyle and Alchemy Renewable Energy to develop, own, and operate utility-scale solar assets in the United States. Its end markets are utilities and other contracted power purchasers that buy output under long-term offtake arrangements. The business is asset-heavy because value is tied to project development, financing, construction, and ownership of operating solar assets. Carlyle disclosed that the platform launched with a dozen operating assets and a larger development pipeline, giving it immediate scale as well as forward-build capacity. Public materials show the partnership was announced in 2020 following Carlyle's earlier capital commitment and that the platform secured project-level contracted milestones such as the Twittys Creek solar power purchase agreement.

profileSubsector: Utility-scale solar, storage, and power development
ownershipDetails: Firm: Carlyle | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Copia Power develops utility-scale solar, battery storage, and related power infrastructure projects in the western United States. Its customers include utilities, corporate buyers, and large-load power users that need contracted or dedicated generation and storage capacity. The business follows an asset-heavy development model because it originates, finances, procures, and advances physical infrastructure assets rather than acting only as an advisory platform. Company materials state that the platform was founded by Carlyle in 2021 and, by late 2025, had more than 1,500 MW and 4,469 MWh operating or under construction, with a much larger multi-sector development pipeline. Carlyle remains the disclosed sponsor, and Copia also announced a $300 million corporate credit facility in 2024 to accelerate development and procurement.

profileSubsector: Crude oil midstream and carbon capture infrastructure
ownershipDetails: Firm: Carlyle | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Crescent Midstream provides crude oil gathering, transportation, and related midstream services in the Gulf of Mexico and Louisiana. Its customers are offshore producers and shippers that require pipeline and handling infrastructure to move production from offshore and coastal systems. The business is asset-heavy and largely infrastructure-based because it depends on ownership and operation of pipelines and related facilities rather than a trading or brokerage model. Public company materials state that Crescent operates more than 1,200 miles of pipelines, serves more than 80 deepwater platforms, and handles nearly 200 million barrels per year across offshore and onshore segments. Public disclosures identify the company as a Carlyle-backed platform, while recent company announcements also show expansion into carbon capture and sequestration infrastructure in Louisiana.
historicalMilestones: 2020: Public leadership materials state that Jeremiah Ashcroft became Chief Executive Officer.

ownershipDetails: Firm: Nuvve Holding Corp. (via Fermata Energy II LLC) | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Fermata Energy develops vehicle-to-grid software and integration capabilities that enable bidirectional charging and grid services from electric vehicles. Its customers include fleet operators, site hosts, and power-market participants that use electric vehicles and charging assets to reduce energy costs or provide grid-support services. The model is more technology- and services-oriented than a traditional generation asset owner, but it still depends on deployment of charging hardware and market participation arrangements at customer sites. Company materials identify 2010 as the founding year, and Fermata became one of the earlier public vehicle-to-grid specialists in the United States market. Carlyle led a financing round announced in 2022, while Nuvve announced in 2025 that it had acquired Fermata Energy assets through Fermata Energy II LLC.
historicalMilestones: 2010: Company materials identify Fermata Energy as founded in 2010.

Firm: Carlyle | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: NineDot develops, owns, and operates distributed battery energy storage systems designed for the New York City metropolitan area. Its customer base includes the electric grid and local communities, with projects positioned to support reliability, resiliency, and distributed energy needs in dense urban load pockets. The business follows an asset-heavy infrastructure model centered on development, financing, construction, and operation of front-of-the-meter storage projects. Company materials state that the platform was founded in 2015, now has more than 80 employees, and is building a portfolio across the New York metro area. In January 2024 NineDot announced a $225 million equity financing led by Manulife Investment Management alongside Carlyle to fund construction and operation of its battery storage pipeline.

ownershipDetails: Firm: Carlyle | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Tillman Infrastructure develops, owns, and operates wireless tower sites in the United States. Its customers are wireless carriers and related communications tenants that need tower space and site infrastructure to expand network coverage and capacity. The business is asset-heavy and contracted because returns depend on the ownership, development, and leasing of macro tower assets under multi-year tenant agreements. Company materials state that Tillman was founded in October 2016, built its first tower in May 2017, and by 2024 had more than 2,000 macro tower sites in service with several thousand more in development. Carlyle announced in 2022 that it would commit up to $1 billion to accelerate investment in the platform.

companyOverview: Wyyerd Fiber builds and operates fiber-to-the-premise broadband networks in the southwestern United States. Its customers include residential users, businesses, and community institutions that need high-speed internet and related communications services. The business is asset-heavy because growth depends on network construction, last-mile deployment, and operation of owned fiber infrastructure rather than a resale-only service model. Company materials show an operating footprint in Arizona and California, while financing announcements in 2025 highlighted continued expansion into Tucson and the Phoenix metro area. Public disclosures identify Carlyle and Columbia Capital among the platform's equity sponsors, and Wyyerd announced debt financings in 2023 and 2025 to accelerate network expansion.

companyOverview: Aira Solar is a utility-scale solar development in Alberta near Seven Persons and the Medicine Hat area. The project is intended to supply large-scale renewable generation into the Alberta market, although the final offtake structure has not been publicly disclosed. The model is asset-heavy because value depends on permitting, financing, construction, and operation of a 450 MWac solar facility rather than on a services-only platform. Public project materials describe approximately 1.15 million solar panels on roughly 4,480 to 4,500 acres, making it one of the larger solar developments publicly identified in Alberta. The project is presented on Horizon New Energy's project list following CIP's launch of that Canadian renewables platform in 2023.

ownershipDetails: Firm: BKV Corporation (via BKV dCarbon Ventures / BKV dCarbon Project, LLC) | Fund: Not publicly disclosed. | Ownership Interest: 51%
Firm: Copenhagen Infrastructure Partners (via C Squared Solutions, Inc.) | Fund: Energy Transition Fund I | Ownership Interest: 49%
companyOverview: The BKV-CIP Joint Venture is a carbon capture and sequestration platform formed to develop, build, and operate CCS projects in the United States. Its customer base includes industrial emitters and energy producers that need capture, transportation, and permanent sequestration solutions for carbon dioxide emissions. The platform is asset-heavy because it depends on project development, physical capture and transport infrastructure, and underground sequestration assets rather than advisory-only services. BKV announced the venture in 2025 with up to $500 million of capital support, and subsequent SEC filings identified the operating vehicle and disclosed that BKV holds 51 percent while CIP holds 49 percent through Energy Transition Fund I. The public record indicates the joint venture is intended to scale a pipeline of CCS projects anchored in BKV's upstream and carbon-management footprint.

ownershipDetails: Firm: Copenhagen Infrastructure Partners | Fund: CI V | Ownership Interest: 100%
companyOverview: Beehive is a grid-scale battery energy storage project in Peoria, Arizona. The asset serves Arizona Public Service and the regional grid through storage capacity and tolling-style services under a long-term contracted structure. The business model is asset-heavy because returns depend on ownership and operation of a 250 MW four-hour battery system with a total 1 GWh of storage capacity. Public project materials show that EDF originated the project and signed a 20-year agreement with APS in 2024, and CIP acquired full ownership through CI V in 2025. EDF's project disclosures also identify a targeted 2026 operational date.

ownershipDetails: Firm: Copenhagen Infrastructure Partners | Fund: CI IV | Ownership Interest: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Buffalo Plains Wind Farm is a large onshore wind project in Alberta near Lomond. The asset supplies utility-scale renewable generation into the Alberta market, with project materials emphasizing its role as a major grid-connected wind facility in the province. The operating model is asset-heavy because value is tied to the development, construction, and operation of a 495 MW wind farm rather than an outsourced management model. Public sources describe 83 turbines and characterize Buffalo Plains as one of the largest single-phase wind farms in Canada. CIP disclosed that CI IV acquired the project and took final investment decision in 2022, with public materials later indicating commercial operation around the end of 2024.

profileSubsector: Solar PV and storage development portfolio
ownershipDetails: Not publicly disclosed.
companyOverview: Candela Renewables develops utility-scale solar and storage projects across multiple United States power markets. Its end markets are utilities, corporate buyers, and other wholesale offtakers that contract for renewable generation and storage capacity from development-stage and operating projects. The business is asset-heavy because it creates value through land origination, interconnection, permitting, and project development rather than through a services-only advisory model. Public company materials state that Candela was founded in 2018 and currently lists 27 projects totaling 11.2 GW across five regional transmission organizations. Public records show that Naturgy acquired a large Candela portfolio in 2021 and that later Kentucky filings identify a transfer of at least one former Candela project to a CIP-managed CI V entity, but the current ownership structure for the full portfolio is not publicly disclosed.
